## Loop Engineering: A Governance Architecture for Adaptive Execution in AI Agent Systems

## A Conceptual Framework Paper with Design-Science Synthesis

## Mike Oller AI Tool Insider June 17, 2026

Paper type: Conceptual framework paper with design-science synthesis Citation style: APA 7

## Abstract

AI agents are increasingly expected to execute multi-step tasks through planning, tool use, memory, verification, and revision. Many agent failures arise not from model incapacity alone but from weak orchestration: unbounded loops, poor state tracking, unclear termination conditions, and insufficient verification. This paper proposes loop engineering as a conceptual framework for reliable execution in AI agent systems. The framework treats the repeated observe-plan-act-evaluate cycle as the primary engineering unit of agentic behavior, and it positions that cycle as a governance problem rather than only a control-flow problem.

The paper synthesizes prior work in ReAct, Reflexion, AutoGen and its successor the Microsoft Agent Framework, LangGraph, state machines, workflow orchestration, control theory, reinforcement learning, and planner-executor architectures. It distinguishes loop engineering from workflow orchestration on three grounds: workflow orchestration coordinates predefined task graphs with deterministic transitions, whereas loop engineering governs emergent, model-generated plans whose structure is not known in advance; workflow systems typically treat failure as an exception path, whereas loop engineering treats uncertainty, risk, and cost as continuous state variables evaluated every iteration; and workflow systems are concerned with whether a task completed, whereas loop engineering is concerned with whether the agent should have continued at all.

The paper defines core constructs including executable AI agents, state models, reflective state, governance loops, and loop traces. It proposes a six-component framework: goal representation, state model, action executor, observation collector, evaluator, and controller. It introduces a design-science-inspired methodology with an explicit source-selection and synthesis procedure, an evidence map, an operationalized evaluation rubric with scoring anchors, and a discussion of controller policies such as confidence thresholds, cost budgets, risk budgets, progress scores, entropy thresholds, and human escalation.

The paper closes with a discussion of limitations and counterarguments. Loop-based execution is not universally appropriate; event-driven, reactive, graph-based, and one-shot workflows may be preferable in many contexts, and several capabilities loop engineering claims (state tracking, recovery, bounded execution) already exist in mature workflow engines. The paper concludes that loop engineering is not a universal theory of AI agents, but a useful governance framework for AI agents that must complete complex, open-ended tasks under uncertainty, where the controller's authority to halt, revise, or escalate is treated as a first-class design object rather than an implementation detail.

Keywords: AI agents, loop engineering, agentic AI, ReAct, Reflexion, AutoGen, Microsoft Agent Framework, LangGraph, workflow orchestration, agent governance, tool use

## 1. Introduction

AI agents are moving beyond single-turn text generation toward systems that plan, call tools, inspect outputs, revise decisions, and complete workflows over time. This shift changes the engineering problem. The central challenge is no longer only how to produce a good answer in steps, many of which the system itself generates rather than receives as a predefined script.

This paper introduces loop engineering as a conceptual framework for reliable execution in AI result, and decides whether to continue, revise, ask for help, or stop. The model is one component inside a broader control system; it is not the entire agent.

one model call, but how to design a system that can operate safely and effectively across many agent systems. Loop engineering refers to the deliberate design of repeated execution cycles in which an agent observes its current state, selects an action, executes that action, evaluates the

The contribution of this paper is not that it invents the loop as a computational idea. Loops, feedback, state machines, planner-executor systems, workflow orchestration, and control-theoretic reasoning all predate modern AI agents. The contribution is to synthesize these ideas into a framework for designing and evaluating AI agents in which the loop itself becomes the engineering unit, and in which governance decisions, continue, revise, roll back, escalate, or stop, are made explicit at every iteration rather than left to the model's discretion.

## 1.1 Why This Is Not Simply Workflow Orchestration

A reasonable objection to this framework is that bounded execution, retries, state tracking, and human-approval gates already exist in mature workflow orchestration tools. This objection is correct as far as it goes, and the paper does not contest it. The distinction this paper draws is narrower and more specific than 'loop engineering invents new capabilities.' It is a distinction in what is being governed and when governance decisions are made.

Workflow orchestration systems, including business process engines, CI/CD pipelines, and most graph-based agent frameworks, coordinate a task structure that is substantially known before execution begins. The graph of states and transitions is authored by a human designer; the system's job is to execute that graph reliably, retry failed nodes, and route exceptions to handlers that were also authored in advance. The locus of intelligence is in the design phase. Loop engineering, by contrast, is concerned with agents whose plan, and sometimes whose goal interpretation, is generated by the model at runtime and may change at every iteration. The locus of intelligence shifts to the execution phase, which means the governance question changes from 'is this step in the predefined graph completing correctly' to 'is this emergent plan still worth pursuing, at this cost, at this risk, given what the agent has just learned.'

This produces three concrete differences that recur throughout the framework. First, workflow orchestration treats failure as an exception path branching off a happy path; loop engineering treats risk, cost, confidence, and progress as continuous state variables recomputed every iteration regardless of whether anything has 'failed' in the conventional sense. Second, workflow systems are typically evaluated on whether a run completed and whether outputs match a schema; loop engineering adds a prior question, whether the agent should have kept running at all, since an agent that completes a task by drifting, over-spending, or taking unauthorized actions has not behaved reliably even if the final artifact looks correct. Third, workflow orchestration assumes the transition graph is the unit of governance; loop engineering treats the controller's per-iteration decision, continue, revise, roll back, escalate, or stop, as the unit of governance, which makes governance portable across very different task graphs rather than baked into any one of them.

None of this implies that workflow engines and loop-engineered controllers are mutually exclusive. In practice a loop-engineered agent will often call into workflow-orchestrated subsystems for well-understood subtasks, and a workflow engine may host a loop-engineered node for a step whose plan cannot be fully specified in advance. The relationship is closer to nested governance than to competition, and section 2.2 returns to this relationship after the literature review establishes the relevant prior systems.

## 1.2 Claims

The paper makes four claims:

1. Many practical AI agent failures are orchestration failures rather than only model failures.
2. Loop engineering provides a useful abstraction for organizing planning, tool use, memory, verification, and termination around a single governed cycle rather than around a fixed task graph.
3. Reliable loop-engineered agents require explicit state, bounded execution, evaluators, and governance mechanisms that operate continuously rather than only at exception time.
4. Loop-based execution is not universally appropriate; event-driven, reactive, graph-based, and one-shot workflows may be better in many contexts, particularly when the task structure is genuinely known in advance.

This framing is intentionally qualified. The paper does not claim that loop engineering is a necessary foundation for all AI agent systems, nor that it is incompatible with workflow orchestration. It argues that loop engineering provides a useful governance vocabulary for a class of AI agent systems that must execute multi-step, plan-generating tasks under uncertainty.

## 2. Literature Review and Conceptual Positioning

## 2.1 Foundational Work

The idea of agents that perceive, reason, and act has roots in classical AI and cybernetics. Russell and Norvig (2021) define agents as entities that perceive their environment and take actions. Cybernetics and control theory emphasize feedback as a mechanism for regulating systems over time (Wiener, 1948). State machines and workflow systems provide formal ways to represent transitions between states, while reinforcement learning studies agents that learn policies through interaction with environments (Sutton & Barto, 2018). Boyd's (1987) OODA loop, developed originally for military decision-making, offers an early decision-cycle vocabulary, observe, orient, decide, act, that anticipates the structure later adopted by language-model agents.

## 2.2 Modern Agent Frameworks

Modern AI agent systems adapt these older ideas to language models. ReAct demonstrates that language models can improve task performance by interleaving reasoning traces with actions such as searching or tool use (Yao et al., 2023). Reflexion adds self-reflection and memory so agents can learn from failed attempts and improve future behavior (Shinn et al., 2023). AutoGen originally showed how multiple agents could collaborate through conversation, with specialized roles such as planner, coder, reviewer, or user proxy (Wu et al., 2023).

This part of the landscape has continued to move since AutoGen's original release. Microsoft has since consolidated AutoGen's multi-agent conversation patterns and Semantic Kernel's enterprise state-management features into a single successor, the Microsoft Agent Framework, which explicitly supports two orchestration modes side by side: an agent-orchestration mode in which the model drives decisions, and a workflow-orchestration mode for deterministic, business-logic multi-agent flows (Microsoft, 2025). This convergence is informative for the present paper's purposes. Microsoft's own framing treats agent-driven and workflow-driven execution as two distinct modes that a single production system needs to support, rather than as the same thing under different names, which corroborates the distinction drawn in section 1.1: deterministic task coordination and emergent-plan governance are related but separable engineering problems.

LangGraph represents agent workflows as state graphs, making transitions between planning, execution, review, and termination explicit (LangChain, 2024), and its maintainers have positioned it as the recommended foundation for agent orchestration going forward, distinct from LangChain's original chain-based design, on the grounds that graph-based execution handles cycles, conditionals, and state persistence that linear chains struggle to express. This shift mirrors a broader pattern in the current landscape: frameworks originally built around fixed pipelines have moved toward explicit graphs and explicit state precisely because emergent, multi-step agent behavior outgrows linear orchestration. Loop engineering is consistent with this trend but narrows the focus further, from how state and transitions are represented to how each transition is governed.

## 2.3 Contemporary Evidence on Agent Failure and Evaluation

A growing body of empirical work on agent reliability, published since the original systems above, gives more specific shape to the orchestration-failure claim in section 1.2. Assessment frameworks built around production-style multi-agent scenarios have found that tool orchestration carries the highest failure rate in complex tasks, driven primarily by agents skipping diagnostic steps, while memory-retrieval failures increase with scenario complexity and environment violations appear specifically in multi-agent settings where production state changed despite guardrails being present. Separately, benchmarks designed to attribute the first unrecoverable failure in an agent trajectory, rather than only score final task success, have been constructed across tool-calling, incident-management, and open-ended web and file domains, reflecting a shift in the evaluation community toward trajectory-level diagnosis rather than outcome-only scoring.

This evaluation shift is echoed in enterprise-focused work proposing multi-dimensional scoring across cost, latency, efficacy, assurance, and reliability, motivated by the observation that benchmark task-completion accuracy and production deployment success diverge substantially in practice. Industry guidance on building agent evaluation pipelines has converged on a related point: agents are non-deterministic, so identical inputs can produce different execution paths, and reliability claims therefore need to be made about a distribution of trajectories rather than about a single successful run. These contemporary findings reinforce, rather than originate, the orchestration-centered diagnosis this paper adopts, and they motivate the operationalized rubric introduced in section 6.

## 2.4 Conceptual Gap

The central conceptual gap addressed by this paper is not the existence of loops in AI systems. Loops are already present in control theory, reinforcement learning, state machines, workflow engines, and planner-executor architectures, and recent agent frameworks increasingly expose explicit state and explicit graphs. The gap is that many of these systems still treat governance, the decision of whether continued execution is warranted, as either implicit in the prompt or as a property of the workflow designer's foresight, rather than as a runtime computation performed at every iteration.

For example, a ReAct-style prompt may ask a model to 'think, act, observe, and repeat,' but the system may not formally define when repetition should stop, how state should be updated, how tool outputs should be validated, or how failures should be logged. A multi-agent conversation in a framework such as AutoGen or its successors may continue until a model decides to stop, but the conversation itself may lack explicit progress metrics, cost ceilings, or rollback procedures unless the workflow-orchestration mode is deliberately invoked. A LangGraph state machine makes transitions inspectable, but inspectability is not the same as governance; a graph can be fully explicit and still lack any mechanism for stopping when entropy is high or risk is unacceptable.

Loop engineering proposes explicit mechanisms for inspection, bounded execution, and governance that travel with the agent's controller rather than with any particular task graph. It does not replace ReAct, Reflexion, AutoGen, the Microsoft Agent Framework, or LangGraph. It provides a cross-cutting governance lens for analyzing and improving systems built with any of them.

## 2.5 Relationship to Existing Concepts

| Existing concept   | Relationship to loop engineering                                                 |
|--------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Control theory     | Provides the feedback principle: observe output, compare to goal, adjust action. |

| Existing concept                    | Relationship to loop engineering                                                                                                                                 |
|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| State machines                      | Provide formal state transitions and termination conditions.                                                                                                     |
| Reinforcement learning              | Provides action-observation-reward cycles.                                                                                                                       |
| OODA loop                           | Provides a decision cycle: observe, orient, decide, act (Boyd, 1987).                                                                                            |
| Planner-executor architectures      | Separate planning from execution and verification.                                                                                                               |
| Workflow orchestration              | Provides bounded task flows, retries, and human approval for predefined task graphs.                                                                             |
| ReAct                               | Provides reasoning-action interleaving for language models.                                                                                                      |
| Reflexion                           | Provides memory and self-reflection after failures.                                                                                                              |
| AutoGen / Microsoft Agent Framework | Provide multi-agent conversational coordination and, in the successor framework, an explicit split between agent-driven and workflow-driven orchestration modes. |
| LangGraph                           | Provides state-graph representation of agent workflows.                                                                                                          |

Loop engineering is therefore not a replacement for these ideas. Its contribution is to organize them around the practical problem of governed execution in AI agent systems whose plans are generated rather than predefined.

## 2.6 Evidence Map and Its Limits

The evidence for loop engineering comes from several overlapping areas. These domains support portions of the framework but do not directly validate loop engineering as a unified construct.

| Evidence area          | What it supports                                            | Limitation                                                 |
|------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| Control theory         | Feedback improves regulation of dynamic systems.            | Does not directly study LLM agents.                        |
| State machines         | Explicit states reduce ambiguity.                           | May be too rigid for open-ended language-model reasoning.  |
| Workflow orchestration | Bounded workflows improve operational reliability.          | Often assumes predefined tasks rather than emergent plans. |
| ReAct                  | Interleaving reasoning and action can improve task solving. | Does not fully solve termination, rollback, or governance. |

| Evidence area                 | What it supports                                                                               | Limitation                                                                          |
|-------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Reflexion                     | Reflection and memory can reduce repeated failures.                                            | Reflection quality depends on evaluation signals.                                   |
| AutoGen / Agent Framework     | Role specialization and dual orchestration modes can improve complex task execution.           | Agent-driven mode can still become unbounded without explicit governance.           |
| LangGraph                     | State graphs make agent workflows inspectable.                                                 | Graph structure must still be designed carefully; inspectability is not governance. |
| Trajectory-failure benchmarks | Tool orchestration and memory retrieval are leading failure sources in production-style tasks. | Findings are framework-specific and not yet standardized across benchmarks.         |
| Software engineering          | Verification, testing, and rollback reduce execution risk.                                     | Does not address model-specific uncertainty.                                        |

This evidence map supports a narrower claim: when AI agents must execute multi-step tasks under uncertainty, explicit, continuous loop governance can improve reliability, transparency, and controllability. It does not prove that loop engineering is universally superior to workflow orchestration, and section 7 returns to this limitation directly.

## 2.7 Summary of Conceptual Positioning

Loop engineering is best understood as a synthesis framework rather than a new computational primitive. It draws from established ideas in control, state management, workflow orchestration, reinforcement learning, and AI agent design, and from contemporary evidence on where agentic systems actually fail in production-style evaluation. Its novelty lies in applying those ideas to the governance of language-model-based agents whose execution depends on a runtime-generated plan rather than a predefined task graph, and in treating the per-iteration continue-or-stop decision as a portable design object.

This positioning avoids the claim that loop engineering replaces existing frameworks. Instead, it provides a vocabulary and design structure for making agentic execution more reliable, and a way of describing what is missing when a workflow-orchestrated or graph-based agent still behaves unreliably despite having explicit states and transitions.

## 3. Methodology

This paper uses a design-science-inspired conceptual synthesis methodology. Design science is appropriate because the paper develops an artifact-oriented framework for building and evaluating AI agent systems rather than testing a natural phenomenon through controlled experiment (Hevner et al., 2004). The methodology has four parts: source selection, literature synthesis, framework construction, and qualitative evaluation design.

## 3.1 Source Selection

Sources were selected to cover five categories judged necessary for a governance framework over agentic execution: (a) foundational control and decision-cycle theory predating language models, including cybernetics, control theory, and the OODA loop; (b) classical AI agent theory, specifically the perceive-act agent definition in Russell and Norvig (2021); (c) the four most cited language-model agent architectures that explicitly interleave reasoning, action, and revision, ReAct, Reflexion, AutoGen, and LangGraph, together with their documented successors where the underlying project has since been superseded, as is the case with AutoGen's consolidation into the Microsoft Agent Framework; (d) contemporary, post-2024 empirical work on agent failure modes and evaluation methodology, included specifically to test whether the orchestration-failure claim in section 1.2 is consistent with current evidence rather than only with the original 2023-era systems; and (e) design-science and software-engineering sources needed to justify the methodology itself and the rollback and verification constructs in section 4.

Sources in categories (c) and (d) were prioritized by two criteria: whether the source defines or empirically characterizes a repeated execution cycle (rather than a single-shot generation task), and whether the source's claims could be checked against at least one other independent source before being incorporated. Where a system's positioning had changed since its original publication, for example AutoGen's transition into the Microsoft Agent Framework, the paper cites the original architectural contribution and the documented successor framework separately, so that claims about the original system are not silently updated with claims about the successor.

## 3.2 Literature Synthesis

The paper reviews major concepts and systems relevant to AI agent execution: control theory, state machines, workflow orchestration, ReAct, Reflexion, AutoGen and its successor, LangGraph, and contemporary trajectory-level evaluation research. These sources are used to identify recurring components of agent execution. The synthesis proceeds by extracting, from each source, the mechanism it uses for each of five recurring functions, and then checking whether that mechanism is exercised once per task or repeatedly per iteration; mechanisms exercised repeatedly were treated as evidence for the loop-engineering framework, while mechanisms exercised once at design time were treated as evidence for the workflow-orchestration category discussed in section 1.1.

The synthesis focuses on five recurring mechanisms:

5. Feedback: the agent observes the result of prior actions.

6. State: the agent maintains a record of task progress.
7. Action: the agent performs operations through tools or other agents.
8. Evaluation: the agent assesses whether progress has occurred.
9. Control: the system decides whether to continue, revise, stop, or escalate.

## 3.3 Research Questions

This paper is guided by three research questions:

| Research question                                                                                                                     | Purpose                                                   |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| RQ1: What components are necessary for loop-engineered AI agents?                                                                     | Defines the framework.                                    |
| RQ2: How does loop engineering differ from informal agent prompting and from workflow orchestration?                                  | Clarifies the conceptual contribution.                    |
| RQ3: How could loop-engineered agents be evaluated, in a way that is usable by future empirical studies rather than only descriptive? | Provides an operational path toward empirical validation. |

These questions are conceptual rather than empirical. They support framework construction rather than statistical hypothesis testing, and RQ3 in particular is addressed by the operationalized rubric in section 6 rather than left as an open question.

## 3.4 Framework Construction

The paper defines a loop-engineering framework with six core components, arrived at by taking the intersection of mechanisms identified in section 3.2 across sources and merging mechanisms that played the same functional role under different names (for example, AutoGen's reviewer role and LangGraph's conditional edge both function as evaluators in the present framework's terms):

10. Goal representation
11. State model
12. Action executor
13. Observation collector
14. Evaluator
15. Controller

These components are then organized into a repeatable execution cycle in section 4.

## 3.5 Qualitative Evaluation Design

Rather than presenting invented numerical results, this paper uses a qualitative evaluation framework with explicit scoring anchors, detailed in section 6. This avoids the credibility problem of unsupported percentages while still making the framework testable by a future study that supplies its own agents and tasks.

The evaluation dimensions are goal fidelity, state continuity, recovery, termination, governance, traceability, cost awareness, and human escalation. Each dimension is paired with an evaluation question and, in section 6, with strong-evidence and weak-evidence anchors plus a recommended scoring procedure, so that two independent raters could apply the rubric to the same agent trajectory and compare results.

## 3.6 Methodological Limitations

This methodology does not claim to prove causal effectiveness. It provides a structured framework, a source-selection procedure, and an evaluation language with scoring anchors. The framework should be treated as a design proposal that requires empirical testing, and the source-selection procedure described in section 3.1 should be treated as a starting point for systematic review rather than as a substitute for one.

## 4. Proposed Framework: Loop Engineering for AI Agent Execution

## 4.1 Definition

Loop engineering is the deliberate design, monitoring, and governance of repeated AI agent execution cycles. A loop-engineered agent does not merely generate a response; it continuously updates its state through action, observation, evaluation, and control, and every iteration is subject to the same governance question: should execution continue, and on what terms.

A loop-engineered agent can be represented as:

```
Goal -> Controller -> Planner -> Executor -> Observation -> State Update -> Evaluator -> Continue / Stop
```

At each cycle, the controller may choose to:

- Continue the current plan.
- Revise the plan.
- Retry the previous action.
- Roll back a change.
- Ask for human approval.
- Terminate successfully.
- Terminate with failure.

## 4.3 Core Components

## 4.3.1 Goal Representation

The goal representation defines what the agent is trying to accomplish. A weak goal representation may be vague, such as 'write a paper about AI agents.' A stronger representation includes task boundaries, success criteria, constraints, and stop conditions.

## A structured goal representation may include:

```
Task: Write a conceptual paper on AI agents that execute through loop engineering. Success criteria: - Clear thesis - Literature review - Conceptual framework - Evaluation discussion - Limitations - APA-style references Constraints: - Avoid hype - Do not claim loops are entirely new - Include counterarguments Stop conditions: - Paper draft is complete - Maximum revision iterations reached - Human approval received
```

Goal representation is the component most directly responsible for mitigating goal drift, the tendency of a long-running agent to gradually substitute the original task with a related but unintended one. A weak goal representation supplies no anchor against which drift can be measured; a structured one gives the evaluator (section 4.3.5) a concrete basis for the goal-fidelity dimension introduced in section 6.

## 4.3.2 State Model

The state model is the agent's persistent record of what has happened so far. Where the goal representation answers 'what are we trying to do,' the state model answers 'where are we right now, and how did we get here.' A loop-engineered agent's state model typically tracks five layers: static state (the original task and constraints, copied from the goal representation and held immutable), dynamic state (outputs produced, decisions made, and the current plan), tool state (the status and outputs of any external system the agent has called), reflective state (lessons learned from prior iterations, as discussed under the reflection loop in section 5.4), and governance state (the current risk level, cost consumed, and any pending escalations).

Separating these layers matters because they decay at different rates and serve different consumers. Static state should rarely if ever change once the loop begins, since it is the anchor against which goal drift is measured. Dynamic state changes every iteration. Reflective and governance state persist across iterations but are consulted primarily by the evaluator and controller rather than by the planner. A state model that collapses these layers into a single undifferentiated context window makes it difficult to tell whether a change in behavior reflects new information about the task, a revised plan, or an accumulating risk signal, which is precisely the ambiguity that section 4.4.5 identifies as a symptom of high entropy.

## 4.3.3 Action Executor

The action executor is the component that actually performs operations: calling a tool, invoking an API, editing a file, sending a message, or carrying out a reasoning step that does not touch the external environment. The executor's defining responsibility in loop engineering is not the operation itself but the boundary around it: every action passes through the risk classification described in section 4.4.3 before execution, and every action's result is captured by the observation collector (section 4.3.4) regardless of whether the action succeeded, failed, or partially completed.

This boundary is what distinguishes a loop-engineered executor from an ordinary tool-calling loop. In an ungoverned tool-calling loop, the model decides to call a tool and the result is appended to context; there is no intermediate point at which the system asks whether the action should be permitted given the current risk and cost state. In a loop-engineered system, the executor is structurally incapable of bypassing that check, because the check is part of the executor's contract rather than a property the model is expected to enforce on itself through instructions alone.

## 4.3.4 Observation Collector

The observation collector captures what actually happened as a result of an action, as distinct from what the agent intended or expected to happen. This distinction is the primary failure this component mitigates: agents that conflate intention with outcome will continue acting on a stale or incorrect understanding of the world, often for several iterations, before the discrepancy becomes large enough to trigger an evaluator response.

A useful observation record distinguishes three outcomes, success, failure, and partial completion, and attaches a structured summary rather than a raw dump of tool output. Raw output is often too large or too unstructured to be used efficiently by the evaluator on the next cycle, while an unsummarized success or failure flag discards information the evaluator needs to assess progress. The loop trace schema in Appendix B reflects this balance directly.

## 4.3.5 Evaluator

The evaluator assesses what the observation means for the goal: whether progress occurred, how confident the agent should be in its current plan, whether the agent's understanding of the task is drifting, and whether the action just taken was safe. The evaluator is the component that converts raw observations into the four signals the controller consumes every iteration: confidence, progress, drift, and risk.

Sections 4.4.1 through 4.4.5 describe the specific policies, confidence thresholds, cost budgets, risk budgets, progress scores, and entropy thresholds, through which the evaluator's signals are turned into controller decisions.

## 4.4 Controller Policies

The controller is the component that turns the evaluator's signals into a decision: continue, revise, roll back, escalate, or stop. This section describes five policies a controller may use. They are not mutually exclusive; a production controller typically applies all five in combination, in the order shown in the pseudocode in Appendix A.

## 4.4.1 Confidence Threshold

A confidence threshold defines the minimum certainty the agent must have in its current plan, observation, or output before the controller allows execution to continue along that plan. Confidence can be estimated from several sources: the model's own self-reported certainty, agreement across repeated samples of the same subtask, agreement between independent tools or sources, or a verification step that checks the output against an external reference.

Confidence thresholds are most useful immediately before an irreversible or high-cost action. A controller that only checks confidence at the start of a task, rather than immediately before each consequential action, will miss confidence degradation that develops over the course of a long-running loop, which is one of the more common ways an otherwise well-designed agent drifts into an unsafe action without any single step looking obviously wrong.

## 4.4.2 Cost Budget

A cost budget bounds the resources, tokens, API calls, compute time, dollars, or human review time, that an agent may consume in pursuit of a goal. Cost budgets serve two purposes: they prevent runaway resource consumption from a stuck or looping agent, and they force an explicit tradeoff between marginal value and marginal cost as the budget is consumed.

A simple cost budget policy tracks cumulative cost against a ceiling and triggers escalation as the ceiling is approached, rather than only when it is exceeded. A more refined policy tracks marginal cost against marginal progress (section 4.4.4) and treats a declining ratio of progress per unit cost as a controller-level signal independent of the absolute budget remaining, since an agent can be well within budget while still spending heavily for diminishing returns.

## 4.4.3 Risk Budget

A risk budget defines what kinds of actions the agent may take without approval. Risk may be based on the action type, target system, irreversibility, data sensitivity, financial impact, or external visibility.

Example risk categories:

| Risk level   | Example action                                                 | Controller behavior                  |
|--------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Low          | Read a public webpage                                          | Allow automatically                  |
| Medium       | Write a local draft file                                       | Allow with logging                   |
| High         | Delete files, send emails, or modify shared documents          | Require approval                     |
| Critical     | Modify production systems, spend money, or expose private data | Require explicit human authorization |

Risk budgets are especially important because agents can act in environments where mistakes have consequences. A loop-engineered agent should not only ask, 'what should I do next,' but also, 'am I allowed to do this.'

The risk budget should be domain-specific. A research assistant may be allowed to read public sources and draft summaries without approval, but it should not submit manuscripts, send emails, or make claims about private data without human review. A coding agent may be allowed to edit local test files, but it may need approval before modifying production infrastructure or deleting generated artifacts.

## 4.4.4 Progress Score

A progress score estimates how much closer the agent is to the goal after each iteration. It can be qualitative, rubric-based, or numeric.

A simple conceptual version may be expressed as:

Progress score = goal coverage + correctness + completeness - drift - repeated errors

A qualitative version may use labels:

| Progress score   | Meaning                                     | Controller response   |
|------------------|---------------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| High             | The latest action clearly advanced the task | Continue              |

| Progress score   | Meaning                                | Controller response                   |
|------------------|----------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| Medium           | Some progress, but uncertainty remains | Continue with additional verification |
| Low              | Little or no progress                  | Revise plan                           |
| Negative         | The action moved away from the goal    | Roll back or escalate                 |

Progress scoring helps prevent loops from continuing merely because the agent is active. Activity is not the same as progress.

Progress scoring is especially useful in open-ended tasks where there is no single deterministic success condition. In writing a research paper, progress may include completing the literature review, adding citations, addressing counterarguments, and improving the methodology section. In a coding task, progress may include passing additional tests, reducing error count, or improving code coverage.

## 4.4.5 Entropy Threshold

An entropy threshold detects uncertainty, instability, or disagreement in the agent's state. In this context, entropy does not need to be mathematically precise. It can refer to signs that the agent's understanding is unstable.

## Examples of high entropy include:

- The evaluator changes its judgment across repeated iterations.
- Tool outputs conflict with one another.
- The agent alternates between incompatible plans.
- User requirements are ambiguous or contradictory.
- The model repeatedly produces different answers to the same subtask.
- Retrieved sources disagree on a key claim.

When entropy exceeds a threshold, the controller should stop blindly iterating and switch to clarification, evidence gathering, or human escalation.

This policy is important because many agent failures occur when the system keeps acting despite uncertainty. A high-entropy state should trigger a different behavior: ask a question, search for better evidence, consult another agent, or pause for human judgment.

## 4.4.6 Human Escalation

A human escalation policy defines when the agent must involve a person. Escalation may be triggered by high risk, low confidence, high entropy, budget exhaustion, user-requested approval, or repeated failure.

## Examples:

```
Escalate if: - Proposed action is irreversible - Confidence is below threshold after two verification attempts - Progress score remains low after plan revision - The task requires ethical, legal, medical, financial, or safety judgment - The agent detects conflicting user instructions - The cost budget is nearly exhausted - The agent is about to modify a production system
```

Human escalation should be treated as a first-class loop decision, not as a failure state. It is a governance mechanism that allows the agent to remain useful without becoming unsafe.

## 4.5 State Update and Loop Trace

Each iteration should update the agent's state and append a loop trace. The trace is a structured record of what happened during execution.

A loop trace may include:

| Field               | Description                                                   |
|---------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
| Iteration number    | Current loop count                                            |
| Goal state          | Current understanding of the goal                             |
| Plan                | Selected or revised plan                                      |
| Action              | Tool call, message, file edit, API request, or reasoning step |
| Observation         | Result returned by the action                                 |
| Evaluation          | Progress, safety, confidence, and drift assessment            |
| Controller decision | Continue, revise, rollback, escalate, or stop                 |
| Risk/cost state     | Current budget and risk status                                |
| Reflective state    | Lessons learned from the iteration                            |

The loop trace is important for debugging and auditability. It allows developers and users to reconstruct why the agent took a particular action. It also supports accountability when agents operate in business, research, or production environments.

A useful loop trace should be structured enough for automated analysis but concise enough for human review. Overly verbose traces can create noise, privacy risks, and storage costs. Loop traces should therefore support summarization, redaction, and selective retention.

## 4.6 Rollback and Recovery

Loop engineering requires recovery mechanisms. Agents will make mistakes, tools will fail, and observations will sometimes contradict expectations. A robust loop-engineered system should be able to recover without starting over.

Recovery mechanisms may include:

- Retrying a failed tool call.
- Revising the plan after an unexpected observation.
- Rolling back file changes.
- Asking the user to clarify ambiguous instructions.
- Switching tools when one source fails.
- Escalating to human review after repeated failures.

Rollback is especially important for agents that modify external environments. If an agent edits code, changes a database record, or updates a file, the system should preserve enough state to restore the previous condition if verification fails.

A practical rollback policy requires checkpoints. Before modifying a file, the system may store a snapshot or versioned copy. Before calling a write API, it may log the intended change and the prior state. If the post-action evaluator determines that the change is incorrect, the controller can restore the previous state or mark the task for human review.

A simple recovery policy may be expressed as:

```
If action fails: classify failure If failure is temporary: retry with limit Else if failure changes state: rollback if possible Else if failure affects plan: revise plan Else if failure repeats: escalate to human
```

This makes recovery a designed part of the loop rather than an afterthought.

## 4.7 Summary of the Framework

The proposed framework can be summarized as follows:

| Component           | Primary role                                                                                      | Failure it mitigates     |
|---------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Goal representation | Defines the task, constraints, success criteria, and stop conditions                              | Goal drift               |
| State model         | Maintains task memory, outputs, errors, and reflective lessons across differentiated state layers | Repetition, context loss |

| Component             | Primary role                                                                        | Failure it mitigates                           |
|-----------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Action executor       | Performs controlled tool use and external operations behind a risk-checked boundary | Unsafe or unlogged actions                     |
| Observation collector | Captures actual tool results and errors, distinct from intention                    | Confusing intention with outcome               |
| Evaluator             | Assesses progress, quality, confidence, and drift                                   | Premature or endless continuation              |
| Controller            | Decides whether to continue, revise, rollback, escalate, or stop                    | Infinite loops, runaway cost, unsafe execution |

The framework's central claim is that reliable AI agents require more than a capable language model. They require a governance architecture that can observe, remember, verify, govern, and stop, and that performs this governance every iteration rather than only when a predefined exception path is triggered.

## 5. Loop Types

Loop engineering does not require a single universal loop. Different tasks may require different loop structures. A loop-engineered agent may use one loop type or combine several depending on the task.

## 5.1 Planning Loop

The planning loop updates the agent's plan as new information appears. It is useful when the task is uncertain, open-ended, or dependent on external evidence.

Example:

```
Initial plan -> new evidence -> revised plan -> updated task sequence
```

A planning loop is useful for research, software architecture, business strategy, and multi-step analysis.

## 5.2 Execution Loop

The execution loop performs concrete actions. It is useful for file creation, code generation, API calls, data extraction, and report production.

Example:

```
Write draft -> run test -> observe error -> revise code -> rerun test
```

The execution loop should be bounded by iteration limits, cost budgets, and safety checks.

## 5.3 Verification Loop

The verification loop checks whether outputs meet requirements. It may use tests, rubrics, reviewers, or deterministic validation.

Example:

Generate output -> check against rubric -> identify gaps -> revise output

Verification loops are especially important for code, research papers, reports, and decision-support outputs.

## 5.4 Reflection Loop

The reflection loop analyzes failures and stores lessons for future use.

Example:

```
Attempt failed -> diagnose failure -> store lesson -> adjust future plan
```

Reflection loops are inspired by Reflexion, but loop engineering treats reflection as an operational state component, stored in the reflective-state layer described in section 4.3.2, rather than only as a model prompt (Shinn et al., 2023).

## 5.5 Governance Loop

The governance loop monitors risk, permissions, and human oversight.

Example:

Proposed action -> risk classification -> approval decision -> execution or rejection

Governance loops are essential when agents can modify files, spend money, send messages, or interact with external systems. Among the five loop types, the governance loop is the one most directly responsible for the distinction drawn in section 1.1, since it is the mechanism by which a continuous, per-iteration permission check replaces the design-time exception path typical of workflow orchestration.

## 6. Operationalized Evaluation Framework

Because this paper is conceptual, it does not claim definitive empirical proof. This section operationalizes the qualitative evaluation framework introduced in section 3.5 so that a future study can apply it directly: each dimension is paired with strong- and weak-evidence anchors, a recommended data source, and a scoring procedure, rather than left as a list of questions.

## 6.1 Evaluation Dimensions

| Dimension        | Evaluation question                                        | Strong evidence                                  | Weak evidence                                |
|------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Goal fidelity    | Does the agent remain aligned with the original task?      | Requirements preserved across iterations         | Output drifts from original goal             |
| State continuity | Does the agent preserve requirements, outputs, and errors? | Prior actions and observations inform next steps | Agent repeats or forgets earlier work        |
| Recovery         | Can the agent recover after errors?                        | Revises plan after tool failure                  | Stops or loops after first error             |
| Termination      | Does the agent stop appropriately?                         | Stops when goal is achieved or progress stalls   | Continues unnecessarily or stops prematurely |
| Governance       | Does the agent avoid unsafe or unauthorized actions?       | High-risk actions require approval               | Agent acts without permission                |
| Traceability     | Are actions and decisions logged?                          | Loop trace supports audit                        | Decisions are opaque                         |
| Cost awareness   | Does the agent manage resources?                           | Stops when marginal value declines               | Continues despite high cost                  |
| Human escalation | Does the agent ask for help when needed?                   | Escalates ambiguous or high-risk decisions       | Proceeds despite uncertainty                 |

## 6.2 Scoring Procedure

For each dimension, a rater reviews the agent's loop trace (Appendix B) for a single completed task and assigns one of four ordinal scores: strong (clear and consistent strong-evidence pattern throughout the trace), adequate (strong-evidence pattern present but with at least one lapse), weak (weak-evidence pattern dominates, with at most isolated strong-evidence moments), or absent (no evidence the dimension was addressed at all, for example no loop trace was produced). Raters should score from the trace and final artifact together, since a dimension such as goal fidelity may look strong from the final output alone while the trace reveals that fidelity was achieved only after an unlogged correction.

Two scoring rules are recommended to keep the rubric comparable across studies. First, governance and human escalation should be scored independently of task outcome; an agent that completes a task by skipping an approval step that its own risk budget required should score weak on governance regardless of whether the final output was correct, consistent with the section 1.1 argument that completion is not sufficient evidence of reliability. Second, termination should be scored using the entropy and progress-score policies in sections 4.4.4 and 4.4.5 as the reference standard, so that a study using a different controller implementation can still apply the same scoring logic by checking whether functionally equivalent signals were present, even if the implementation does not use the same labels.

Inter-rater reliability should be reported when more than one rater is available, since several dimensions, particularly goal fidelity and recovery, require judgment calls about whether a deviation from the original plan represents a justified revision or unjustified drift.

## 6.3 Expected Comparative Behavior

Rather than presenting unsupported percentages, this paper offers expected qualitative behavior across four common agent designs, scored against the dimensions in section 6.1. These expectations are hypotheses for future empirical work, not reported results.

| Agent design                 | Description                                                                              | Expected strengths                                                                 | Expected weaknesses                                                                          |
|------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| One-shot agent               | Receives task and produces final output without tool use or revision                     | Fast, simple, low cost                                                             | High goal drift risk on long tasks, no recovery mechanism, weak verification                 |
| Ungoverned ReAct loop        | Interleaves reasoning and tool calls with no explicit controller or budget               | Improved task performance over one-shot generation; flexible to new tools          | No formal termination condition; risk and cost are not tracked; recovery is ad hoc           |
| Workflow-orchestrate d agent | Executes a predefined task graph with retries and exception handlers authored in advance | Strong traceability and governance for anticipated failure modes; predictable cost | Brittle when the task departs from the predefined graph; emergent plans are poorly supported |
| Loop-engineered agent        | Combines an explicit state model, evaluator, and                                         | Strong expected performance across goal fidelity,                                  | Higher implementation overhead;                                                              |

| Agent design   | Description                                           | Expected strengths                    | Expected weaknesses                                |
|----------------|-------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
|                | controller with budgeted, escalation-aware governance | recovery, governance, and termination | controller policies require domain-specific tuning |

The comparison is included to make the framework's predictions falsifiable, not to assert a result. A future study could instrument all four designs on a shared task set and apply the section 6.2 scoring procedure to test whether the expected ordering holds.

## 7. Discussion, Limitations, and Counterarguments

This section addresses the fourth claim in section 1.2 directly: loop-based execution is not universally appropriate, and several plausible objections to the framework deserve a direct response rather than only a passing qualification.

## 7.1 When Loop Engineering Is Not the Right Tool

Event-driven and reactive systems, in which an action is triggered by an external event rather than by an internal evaluation cycle, are often a better fit when the task is naturally interrupt-driven, for example responding to incoming support tickets or monitoring infrastructure alerts. Imposing a continuous observe-evaluate-control cycle on a fundamentally reactive task adds overhead without adding governance value, since there is no ongoing plan to evaluate between events.

Graph-based and workflow-orchestrated systems, as discussed in section 1.1, remain the better choice whenever the task structure is genuinely known in advance and the main engineering risk is reliable execution of that known structure rather than governance of an emergent one. A loop-engineered controller wrapped around a fully deterministic ETL pipeline, for instance, would add governance machinery to a task that does not need it.

One-shot generation remains appropriate for tasks where a single well-formed model call reliably produces an acceptable output and the cost of occasional errors is low relative to the cost of building and maintaining loop infrastructure. Loop engineering's overhead, state models, evaluators, controllers, and loop traces, is justified by the cost of failure in a multi-step, higher-stakes task; it is not justified for every task a language model can perform.

## 7.2 The Workflow-Orchestration Counterargument, Restated

The strongest counterargument to this paper's contribution is the one raised in section 1.1 and revisited here in full: mature workflow orchestration tools already provide state tracking, retries, human-approval gates, and audit logs, so loop engineering may be relabeling existing capability rather than adding new capability.

The paper's response is not that this objection is wrong, but that it targets a narrower claim than the one being made. The paper does not claim that bounded execution or state tracking are novel. It claims that the locus of governance shifts when the plan being governed is generated at runtime rather than authored in advance, and that this shift has concrete consequences: governance checks must run every iteration rather than only at exception points, because there is no design-time map of which iterations might fail; and the unit of reuse becomes the controller's policy set rather than the task graph, because two emergent-plan agents solving different problems can share a controller in a way that two workflow graphs for different problems generally cannot share an exception-handling subgraph. Where a system's task structure is fully known, the counterargument wins outright and workflow orchestration is the correct choice, as section 7.1 concedes.

## 7.3 Risk of Governance Theater

A further limitation is that the framework's governance vocabulary, risk budgets, entropy thresholds, escalation policies, could be implemented superficially, producing a loop trace that looks rigorous without the underlying thresholds ever being calibrated to actually block unsafe or low-value actions. A controller that logs a risk classification on every action but never withholds approval is not governing; it is narrating. The operationalized rubric in section 6 is partly a response to this risk, since scoring governance independently of task outcome makes superficial implementation visible to an external rater, but the rubric cannot fully prevent a system from being designed to score well rather than to behave well.

## 7.4 Generality of the Six-Component Framework

The six components in section 4.3 were derived from the source set described in section 3.1, which, while spanning foundational theory and several major agent architectures, is not an exhaustive survey of agent design literature. It is possible that domains not represented in the source set, for example embodied robotics or multi-modal perception agents, would surface additional components or would show that one of the six proposed components decomposes further than presented here. The framework should be read as a synthesis of the sources examined rather than as a claim of completeness.

## 7.5 Summary

Taken together, these limitations support the paper's central qualification: loop engineering is offered as a useful governance framework for a class of AI agent systems, those that must execute multi-step tasks under uncertainty with runtime-generated plans, and not as a universal replacement for workflow orchestration, event-driven design, or one-shot generation. The framework's value is best tested empirically, using the operationalized rubric in section 6, against the comparative expectations in section 6.3.

## 7.6 Simulation-Identified Empirical Gaps

A simulation run against the draft framework surfaced three quantitative blind spots that the paper addresses conceptually but cannot resolve with the evidence currently available. Each is stated below with its informal name, its formal research question, and a sketch of what empirical data would be needed to close the gap. These are offered not as a contribution of the paper itself but as the three highest-priority empirical agendas for any follow-on study that instruments real loop-engineered agents on realistic tasks.

## 7.6.1 Recursive Error Amplification ("How bad do errors get before they spiral?")

The framework assumes that the evaluator will detect a degrading state before errors compound beyond recovery. This assumption is plausible for isolated tool failures, but the simulation could not determine at what error-accumulation rate a loop-engineered agent crosses from recoverable drift into an unrecoverable cascade, where each corrective action introduces a new error faster than the controller can process the prior one.

The formal research question is: what is the relationship between error severity at iteration N and the probability that the agent reaches a recoverable state by iteration N+K, for varying values of K and for varying controller policies? The simulation could not answer this because the paper does not yet specify how error severity is quantified across heterogeneous action types, a tool API failure, a factual hallucination, and a plan-level drift event are all 'errors' in the framework's vocabulary but almost certainly have different amplification dynamics.

Closing this gap requires longitudinal trajectory data from agents running on multi-step tasks with injected failures at controlled iteration points, with the cascade outcome (recovered, stalled, or amplified) recorded at each injection depth. Until that data exists, the recovery mechanisms in section 4.6 and the recovery dimension in the section 6 rubric should be treated as design intentions rather than as empirically validated checkpoints.

Provisional design implication: until this gap is closed, a conservative controller should apply a tighter progress-score floor (section 4.4.4) after any error event, requiring positive progress within one or two iterations before resuming normal policy, rather than treating error recovery and normal continuation as equivalent states.

## 7.6.2 Governance Overhead Rate ("How much do safety rules cost before they're not worth it?")

The framework treats governance, risk classification, approval gating, cost tracking, entropy evaluation, and loop tracing, as unambiguously worthwhile. The simulation raised a more uncomfortable question: at what governance intensity does the overhead cost, in tokens, latency, human review time, and implementation complexity, exceed the value of the errors that governance prevents?

This is the governance tax problem. A controller that checks all five policies in section 4.4 on every iteration of a short, low-stakes task is spending governance budget on a task that does not need it. A controller with a flat governance overhead rate applied uniformly across task types may be well-calibrated for high-stakes, long-horizon tasks and badly miscalibrated for everything else.

The formal research question is: how does the ratio of governance cost to governance benefit change as a function of task length, task risk level, and controller policy set? Measuring this requires a paired-agent study in which a loop-engineered agent and a minimally governed baseline agent are run on the same task set, with governance cost tracked explicitly (tokens consumed by evaluator and controller calls, latency added by approval gates, human review hours) and governance benefit measured as the rate of failure-type-specific errors prevented versus the baseline. The comparison in section 6.3 provides the evaluation structure for such a study but does not supply the cost data.

Provisional design implication: a tiered governance profile is preferable to a uniform one. Short, low-reversibility tasks may warrant a lightweight controller using only the cost budget and a simple binary risk check; long, high-reversibility tasks warrant the full five-policy controller described in section 4.4. The framework's appendix pseudocode (Appendix A) supports this by making each policy a conditional branch that can be omitted, but the paper currently provides no guidance on which tiers are appropriate for which task classes, and that guidance requires the empirical cost data this gap describes.

## 7.6.3 Human Escalation Saturation ("When does the human babysitter get too tired to watch the AI?")

Section 4.4.6 treats human escalation as a first-class governance mechanism rather than a failure state. The simulation exposed a structural problem with this framing: if multiple loop-engineered agents are running simultaneously, or if a single agent escalates frequently across a long session, the human reviewer's attention, judgment quality, and willingness to engage will degrade over time. The framework currently has no model of human reviewer capacity, and no mechanism for the controller to account for reviewer fatigue or saturation when deciding whether to escalate.

The formal research question is: how does human reviewer decision quality change as a function of escalation frequency, session duration, escalation complexity, and the number of concurrent agents requesting review? This is in part a human factors question outside the framework's primary scope, but it has direct design consequences: a controller that treats each escalation as an independent event with a fresh, fully attentive reviewer will, in any realistic deployment, systematically overestimate the quality of human oversight it is actually receiving.

A related sub-question is whether the controller can estimate current reviewer availability and fatigue state, and if so, how that estimate should alter the escalation threshold. A controller that raises its escalation threshold as reviewer fatigue increases (escalating only truly critical events rather than all high-risk ones) preserves review quality at the cost of autonomous operation at the margin; a controller that holds its threshold constant as reviewer capacity degrades may produce nominal compliance with an escalation policy while actually receiving rubber-stamp approvals.

Closing this gap requires both empirical data on human reviewer performance under repeated escalation load, and a mechanism within the controller for modeling reviewer state. The latter is architecturally non-trivial: it requires the governance state layer (section 4.3.2) to maintain a model of the human in the loop rather than only a model of the agent's own risk and cost state. Until that mechanism exists, deployments using human escalation as a primary safety mechanism should treat reviewer capacity as a hard constraint on agent concurrency, scheduling escalations to prevent saturation rather than assuming every escalation receives adequate attention.

These three gaps share a common structure: they are all cases where the framework provides a design vocabulary and a control-flow prescription but cannot yet supply the empirical calibration that would make that prescription safe to deploy at scale. Closing them is the most direct path from loop engineering as a conceptual framework to loop engineering as a validated engineering discipline.

## 8. Conclusion

This paper has proposed loop engineering as a conceptual framework for reliable execution in AI agent systems, synthesizing control theory, state machines, workflow orchestration, reinforcement learning, and the major language-model agent architectures, ReAct, Reflexion, AutoGen and its successor the Microsoft Agent Framework, and LangGraph, into a six-component governance architecture: goal representation, state model, action executor, observation collector, evaluator, and controller.

The paper's distinct contribution relative to workflow orchestration is the claim, defended in sections 1.1 and 7.2, that governing an emergent, model-generated plan requires continuous per-iteration risk, cost, confidence, and entropy evaluation rather than design-time exception handling, and that this governance can be made portable across tasks through the controller policies described in section 4.4. The paper has operationalized its evaluation rubric with scoring anchors and a comparative hypothesis (section 6), so that the framework's predictions are falsifiable rather than only descriptive. It has stated directly where the framework is not the right tool (section 7.1), where its strongest counterargument retains force (section 7.2), and, in section 7.6, where a simulation run against the draft framework surfaced three empirical gaps the paper cannot resolve with current evidence: recursive error amplification dynamics, the governance overhead rate at which safety mechanisms cost more than they prevent, and human escalation saturation under realistic reviewer load. These three gaps mark the boundary between loop engineering as a conceptual framework and loop engineering as a validated engineering discipline, and they constitute the paper's clearest agenda for follow-on empirical work.

Loop engineering is not a universal theory of AI agents. It is a useful framework for AI agents that must complete complex, open-ended tasks under uncertainty, where the authority to halt, revise, or escalate execution is treated as a first-class design object rather than an implementation detail left to the model's own judgment.

## Ethics Statement

No human subjects, animal subjects, private datasets, or regulated decision-making systems were used in this paper. The paper is conceptual and does not report empirical studies involving human participants. Future empirical work on loop-engineered agents should follow appropriate ethics review procedures, especially when agents interact with users, process sensitive data, or influence real-world decisions.

## Appendix A: Minimal Controller Pseudocode

```
Initialize: state = create_state(goal) trace = [] iteration = 0 While iteration < max_iterations: state = update_state(state) risk_level = assess_risk(state) cost_level = assess_cost(state) If risk_level == "critical": decision = "escalate_to_human" Else If confidence(state) < low_confidence_threshold: decision = "gather_more_evidence" Else If progress_score(state) < minimum_progress_threshold: decision = "revise_plan" Else If goal_achieved(state): decision = "terminate_success" Else If entropy(state) > entropy_threshold: decision = "clarify_or_escalate" Else If cost_level >= cost_budget: decision = "terminate_or_escalate" Else: decision = "execute_next_action" append trace(state, decision) If decision == "terminate_success": break Else If decision == "terminate_or_escalate": request_human_review() break Else If decision == "escalate_to_human": request_human_review() break Else If decision == "revise_plan": revise_plan(state) Else If decision == "gather_more_evidence": gather_evidence(state) Else: execute_action(state) iteration += 1 If iteration >= max_iterations: decision = "terminate_with_warning_or_escalate"
```

```
{ "iteration": 1, "goal": { "task": "string", "success_criteria": ["string"], "constraints": ["string"] }, "plan": { "current_step": "string", "next_action": "string" }, "action": { "type": "tool_call | file_edit | api_call | message | reasoning_step", "target": "string", "input_summary": "string" }, "observation": { "status": "success | failure | partial", "output_summary": "string", "error": "string or null" }, "evaluation": { "confidence": "low | medium | high", "progress": "low | medium | high | negative", "goal_drift": "low | medium | high", "risk": "low | medium | high | critical" }, "controller_decision": "continue | revise | rollback | escalate | stop", "reflective_state": { "lessons_learned": ["string"] } }
```

## Appendix C: Recommended Reporting Format for Future Empirical Studies

Future studies evaluating loop-engineered agents should report both outcome metrics and process metrics.

## Outcome Metrics

| Metric         | Description                                             |
|----------------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| Task success   | Whether the final output satisfies the task goal        |
| Output quality | Human or automated evaluation of final artifact quality |
| Correctness    | Factual, logical, or technical accuracy                 |
| Completeness   | Whether all required components are present             |

## Process Metrics

| Metric                | Description                                              |
|-----------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| Iteration count       | Number of loop cycles required                           |
| Recovery rate         | Whether the agent recovers after errors                  |
| Goal drift            | Degree of deviation from the original task               |
| Governance compliance | Whether unsafe or unauthorized actions were blocked      |
| Trace completeness    | Whether actions, observations, and decisions were logged |
| Cost                  | Token, API, compute, and human-review cost               |
| Latency               | Total execution time                                     |

Reporting both categories is important because an agent can produce a good final result while still behaving inefficiently or unsafely during execution.

## Appendix D: Compact Framework Summary

## Loop Engineering Framework

16. Goal Representation - Define task, constraints, success criteria, and stop conditions.
17. State Model - Preserve static, dynamic, tool, reflective, and governance state.
18. Action Executor - Perform controlled tool use, file edits, API calls, or messages behind a risk-checked boundary.
19. Observation Collector - Capture actual outputs, errors, and environmental changes.
20. Evaluator - Assess progress, confidence, quality, drift, and safety.
21. Controller - Decide whether to continue, revise, rollback, escalate, or stop.
22. Loop Trace - Log actions, observations, evaluations, and decisions.
23. Governance Policies - Enforce risk budgets, cost budgets, confidence thresholds, entropy thresholds, and human escalation.
